serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of fresh basil is approximately 2–5 g (1–2 tablespoons of whole leaves), used as a herb garnish or flavouring in dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Basil, fresh — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for fresh basil leaves (cr_id F10060050). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 02044 (Spices, basil, fresh) for secondary validation; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables and Herbs — Analogous Estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fresh basil has no direct GI entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 5 (low) by analogy with other non-starchy leafy herbs and vegetables (e.g., parsley, spinach) which have negligible carbohydrate content (2.65 g/100g) and minimal glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per typical 2.5 g serving is effectively 0.0. This estimation is consistent with published literature on non-starchy herbs.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100g) and high potassium (295 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Fresh basil is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content of 4 mg/100g is negligible and its potassium content of 295 mg/100g supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.65 g/100g) and negligible sugar (0.3 g/100g) with estimated low GI (~5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh basil poses no meaningful glycaemic risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its carbohydrate content is minimal at 2.65 g/100g, sugar is only 0.3 g/100g, and the estimated glycaemic load per culinary serving is effectively zero.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and anti-inflammatory polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh basil is cardioprotective; it contains negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and is rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols including rosmarinic acid and eugenol, which may reduce oxidative stress and support endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (rosmarinic acid, eugenol) and high calcium (177 mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh basil may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its documented anti-inflammatory polyphenol content (rosmarinic acid, eugenol) and its calcium content of 177 mg/100g, which supports bone health relevant to RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basil (Ocimum basilicum), commonly known as sweet basil or common basil, is a tender aromatic herb belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae). Fresh basil leaves are bright green, soft, and glossy with a distinctly sweet, slightly peppery, and clove-like aroma and flavour derived from its essential oil content, particularly eugenol and linalool. It is consumed raw as a garnish, blended into sauces such as pesto, or added to salads, soups, and cooked dishes at the end of preparation to preserve its volatile aromatics. Nutritionally, fresh basil is very low in calories (23 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (2.65 g/100g), making it suitable for low-calorie, low-carbohydrate, and ketogenic dietary patterns. It provides a notable amount of protein for a herb (3.15 g/100g), is rich in calcium (177 mg/100g), potassium (295 mg/100g), iron (3.17 mg/100g), vitamin A (264 mcg RAE/100g), and folate (68 mcg DFE/100g). Its sodium content is negligible at 4 mg/100g. Basil contains bioactive polyphenols including rosmarinic acid and eugenol with documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Basil is widely used across Mediterranean, Southeast Asian, and South Asian cuisines and is a staple herb in Italian cooking.

ingredient_synonyms.तुलसी: Hindi name (note: in Hindi, tulsi more precisely refers to holy basil / Ocimum tenuiflorum, but common basil is also called tulsi colloquially)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060050, confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central NDB 02044 (Spices, basil, fresh). Both approved primary sources are in close agreement. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology for non-starchy herbs, clearly documented.
notes: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): fresh basil is a plant food and contains no cholesterol by nature — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): basil is a plant food and contains no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 02044). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 02044 (Spices, basil, fresh) as HPB does not report these micronutrients for this entry; this is documented as an adapted source match. GI is an analogous estimate (~5, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database methodology for non-starchy leafy herbs with negligible carbohydrate content; no direct GI entry exists for fresh basil. Glycemic load per 2.5 g serving = (5 × 2.65 / 100) × 2.5 / 100 = effectively 0.0. Hindi name 'तुलसी' (tulsi) is the closest common Hindi term; strictly, tulsi refers to holy basil (Ocimum tenuiflorum), but sweet basil is colloquially referred to by this term in Hindi-speaking contexts — this distinction is noted.
